findings.title: Running-snapshot carries stale finished_at / duration_ms between retry attempts
findings.description: On a failed/timeout attempt that will be retried, run_task persists the next attempt's 'running' state with the previous attempt's finished_at and duration_ms still on the record. The fields are overwritten when the retry completes, but a concurrent reader of tasks.json between the two _save_task calls would observe a 'running' task with a finished_at already set in the past.
findings.code_snippet:
# Decide whether to retry.
        if result["status"] in {"failed", "timeout"} and attempts < max_retries + 1:
            # Loop: reset status back to running on next iteration via
            # the assignment above.
            continue
        break
findings.reasoning: At line 211-213 the loop sets status='running' and calls _save_task; but target["finished_at"] and target["duration_ms"] were last written at lines 248-249 with the PREVIOUS attempt's terminal values. They are not cleared before the running-save. Trigger: TASKQ_RETRY_LIMIT>0 + first attempt fails/times-out + observer reads tasks.json between line 213's _save_task and the next attempt's line 249 write.
findings.suggested_fix: Clear finished_at and duration_ms (set to None) on the running-save at lines 211-213, or restructure the loop to persist the running state with terminal fields reset.
findings.verify_evidence: executor.py:211-213 sets status='running' but does not reset target['finished_at']/target['duration_ms']; line 248-249 are the only assignments and they write the prior attempt's terminal values.

findings.title: parse-error attempt returns dead '_error' field not consumed by caller
findings.description: _run_once's shlex.parse-failure branch returns a result dict with key '_error':'parse' that no caller (run_task, the persistence code, or cmd_run's JSON emit) reads. Dead field, minor maintenance hazard.
findings.code_snippet:
except ValueError as exc:
        # Malformed shell quoting is a user-input error → not retriable.
        return {
            "status": "failed",
            "exit_code": None,
            "stdout_tail": "",
            "stderr_tail": f"command parse error: {exc}",
            "duration_ms": 0,
            "finished_at": start_iso,
            "_error": "parse",
        }
findings.reasoning: Grep for '_error' across the package returns no consumers. Dead data on the persisted record (when task reaches _save_task with this branch taken).
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the _error key; keep the rest of the parse-error result shape.
findings.verify_evidence: grep -n '_error' 03-development/src/taskq/*.py shows no consumer of the 'parse' sentinel.

findings.title: Read-modify-write in run_task loses updates under concurrent runs (SPEC-scope dependent)
findings.description: Two concurrent run_task invocations each load tasks via load_tasks_or_die, mutate target, and call atomic_write_tasks with the full list. Last writer wins; the other process's terminal result fields are silently overwritten. The SPEC scopes taskq to single-user local CLI use, so this is theoretical for the documented use-case but real for any library consumer or shell-pipelined usage.
findings.reasoning: atomic_write_tasks (store.py:57-75) takes the full list and writes it; concurrent writers race on the same canonical file. Mitigation would require a file lock (fcntl) or per-record rewrite, neither present. Within the documented CLI use this never fires; library usage or background-scheduled pipelines can trigger it.
findings.suggested_fix: Document the single-writer assumption in taskq.store.atomic_write_tasks; or add fcntl.flock around the load+write window if library use is in scope.
findings.verify_evidence: Refuted on SPEC scope: SPEC.md scopes taskq to single-user local CLI; concurrent-run scenario is not in documented contract. Behavior is as designed.

resolution.refute_evidence: SPEC.md §1-§5 describe taskq as a local CLI for a single user. The CLI invocation model is one process at a time on $TASKQ_HOME/tasks.json. Concurrent multi-process runs are outside the documented scope; this is a known property of the design, not a bug.

findings.title: atomic_write_tasks raises uncaught OSError on permission/disk errors
findings.description: open(tmp_path, 'w') and target.parent.mkdir(...) propagate PermissionError / FileNotFoundError / OSError up to cmd_submit / cmd_list / cmd_run without a typed exception; the CLI's except Exception handler in cmd_run catches it and exits 1 with a traceback-printed stderr. SPEC does not define an exit code for IO failure so this is not strictly a SPEC violation, but the failure is presented as an internal-error rather than a clean store-error message.
findings.code_snippet:
target = tasks_json_path()
    target.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    # tmp filename embeds task_id so partial-write detection is unambiguous.
    tmp_name = f"tasks.json.tmp.{task_id}"
    tmp_path = target.parent / tmp_name

    payload = json.dumps(tasks, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
    with open(tmp_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as fh:
findings.reasoning: When TASKQ_HOME is on a read-only filesystem or the parent dir was removed between runs, the call propagates OSError. cmd_submit has no exception handler beyond validate_command; cmd_run's catch-all surfaces 'internal error: ...'. The error message would be more useful as 'store write failed: <reason>'.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) wrap the IO in try/except OSError in atomic_write_tasks and raise a typed StoreWriteError mapped to a clean stderr message, or (b) document that IO failures exit 1 with traceback per current behavior.
findings.verify_evidence: Refuted on SPEC contract: SPEC §3 exit-code matrix lists 0/2/4/1; there is no defined code for IO error and the current exit 1 + stderr is consistent with the 'internal error' bucket. Not a SPEC violation, just a polish item.

resolution.refute_evidence: SPEC.md §3 exit-code matrix has no entry for IO errors; the catch-all 'internal error: ...' stderr + exit 1 in cmd_run is consistent with the documented '其他未預期例外 → exit 1' clause (FR-02). Behavior is as specified.
